evidence_text:
A unified framework for identification of cell-type-specific spatially variable genes in spatial transcriptomic studies. Characterizing cell-type-specific spatially variable genes (SVGs) within tissue context is essential for exploring the landscape of complex biological systems in spatial transcriptomic (ST) studies. In this paper, we present a unified framework, the Mixture of Mixed Models (MMM), designed to directly model RNA count data and identify cell-type-specific SVGs while accounting for cell type composition and correcting for platform effects. Through a comprehensive simulation study and the analyses of eight publicly available ST datasets from various tissues and technologies with different resolutions, we demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of MMM in identifying cell-type-specific SVGs. Notably, our integrative analysis with genome-wide association studies reveals that the cell-type-specific SVGs identified by MMM in a mouse brain study exhibit significant heritability enrichment in brain-related phenotypes. This finding suggests that cell-type-specific SVGs play a vital role in elucidating the mechanisms underlying complex traits and diseases. When applying MMM to analyze a high-resolution Xenium human breast cancer dataset by accounting for the uncertainties in cell segmentation, we find that certain cell-type-specific SVGs may contribute to cell-cell communications, thereby regulating the tissue microenvironment. Furthermore, we show the versatility of MMM by applying it to the 3D tissue models constructed from multiple ST slices, highlighting its utility in analyzing the 3D ST data. Abstract  Characterizing cell-type-specific spatially variable genes (SVGs) within tissue context is essential for exploring the landscape of complex biological systems in spatial transcriptomic (ST) studies. In this paper, we present a unified framework, the Mixture of Mixed Models (MMM), designed to directly model RNA count data and identify cell-type-specific SVGs while accounting for cell type composition and correcting for platform effects. Through a comprehensive simulation study and the analyses of eight publicly available ST datasets from various tissues and technologies with different resolutions, we demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of MMM in identifying cell-type-specific SVGs. Notably, our integrative analysis with genome-wide association studies reveals that the cell-type-specific SVGs identified by MMM in a mouse brain study exhibit significant heritability enrichment in brain-related phenotypes. This finding suggests that cell-type-specific SVGs play a vital role in elucidating the mechanisms underlying complex traits and diseases. When applying MMM to analyze a high-resolution Xenium human breast cancer dataset by accounting for the uncertainties in cell segmentation, we find that certain cell-type-specific SVGs may contribute to cell–cell communications, thereby regulating the tissue microenvironment. Furthermore, we show the versatility of MMM by applying it to the 3D tissue models constructed from multiple ST slices, highlighting its utility in analyzing the 3D ST data.     status  released    display-pdf  yes    is-olf  no    is-manuscript  no    is-preprint  no    is-journal-matter  no    is-scanned  no    is-retracted  no       Received 2025 Feb 25; Accepted 2025 Oct 9; Issue date 2025 Nov 18.      Spatial transcriptomic (ST) technologies ( 1 – 8 ) enable the measurement of gene expression and physical locations simultaneously, providing unprecedented opportunities to reveal the diversity and complexity of biological systems within the tissue context ( 9 ,  10 ). To fully harness the power of ST technologies, it is crucial to characterize spatially variable genes (SVGs) ( 11 – 14 ) in a cell-type-specific manner—essentially, how the gene expression changes across space within a cell type of interest. The cell-type-specific SVGs may exhibit distinct spatial patterns such as clustering and periodicity, reflecting different cell states. These spatial patterns can be attributed to particular tissue structures or local microenvironment niches and indicate certain biological processes in various contexts ( 15 ), including functional programs, pathogenic processes ( 16 ), cell–cell communications ( 17 – 19 ), and tumor ecosystems ( 8 ,  20 ). Further investigations into the cell-type-specific SVGs have great potential to advance our understanding of the mechanisms underlying complex diseases ( 16 ,  21 ), and subsequently help identify potential therapeutic targets.  However, the current limitations of ST technologies present several challenges in characterizing cell-type-specific SVGs. First, cell type composition is a major confounding factor in practice ( 15 ). As the composition of cell types varies spatially, the expression of their marker genes also changes accordingly. Without effectively accounting for this confounding factor, the SVGs identified by most existing methods, such as SpatialDE ( 22 ), SPARK ( 23 ), SPARK-X ( 24 ), and HEARTSVG ( 25 ), may simply be marker genes of a cell type rather than cell-type-specific SVGs. In the ST data with low resolution, such as Slide-seq ( 3 ) and 10x Visium ( 4 ), the gene expression measurements at spatial spots originate from mixtures of multiple cells. Although cell type deconvolution ( 26 ,  27 ) can provide estimates of cell type proportions at each spot, the presence of cell type mixtures complicates the identification of cell-type-specific SVGs. Even in high-resolution ST data like MERFISH ( 1 ), Stereo-seq ( 6 ), 10x Xenium ( 7 ), and 10x Visium HD ( 8 ), uncertainties in cell type identification persist due to diffusion or imperfect cellular segmentation ( 15 ), and overlooking this uncertainty can lead to inflated type I errors and unreplicable results. Second, single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) data are often used as the reference for cell type deconvolution and identification in ST studies. However, the expression profiles observed in ST data may not fully align with scRNA-seq reference data due to platform differences ( 26 ). Without accounting for the platform effects, spurious spatial variations in ST data might be misidentified as SVGs. Third, the expression patterns of cell-type-specific SVGs in real data remain largely unknown and possibly diverse, posing a challenge for using parametric methods that may have limited statistical power. There is still a need for the development of flexible methodologies capable of addressing these challenges and capturing a wide spectrum of spatial patterns.  In this paper, we introduce a unified statistical framework called Mixture of Mixed Models (MMM), standing for the Mixture of Mixed Models, to identify cell-type-specific SVGs. The success of MMM relies on its probabilistic model, which accounts for cell type composition and corrects for platform effects between ST and scRNA-seq data explicitly. In the MMM framework, cell-type-specific gene expression in ST data is decomposed into three components: the average gene expression of the same cell type obtained from scRNA-seq reference data, spatial variations, and nonspatial variations. The statistical significance of spatial variations is examined using a powerful nonparametric test ( 23 ,  28 ) capable of detecting diverse spatial patterns. While methods such as CTSV ( 29 ), C-SIDE ( 15 ), spVC ( 30 ), Celina ( 31 ), and STANCE ( 32 ) can be used to identify cell-type-specific SVGs, MMM achieves superior performance that benefits from our innovations in model and algorithm design (detailed comparison in   SI Appendix ,  Methods  ). Through a comprehensive simulation study, we showed that MMM can produce well-calibrated    P   -values; thus, it can effectively control type I errors and achieve high statistical power. We then applied MMM to eight publicly available ST datasets of different tissues from various species. These datasets were generated by different platforms with a wide range of resolutions, including 10x Visium ( 4 ), Spatial Transcriptomics ( 2 ), 10x Xenium ( 7 ), 10x Visium HD ( 8 ), and Stereo-seq ( 6 ). In our study of the mouse brain, we demonstrated that MMM can identify cell-type-specific SVGs that are replicable across different technologies. We further conducted an integrative analysis with genome-wide association studies (GWASs) and found that the identified cell-type-specific SVGs are significantly enriched in brain-related phenotypes. This suggests that MMM has great potential to elucidate the mechanisms underlying complex traits and diseases. We also used MMM to analyze a high-resolution Xenium human breast cancer dataset by accounting for the uncertainties from imperfect cell segmentation and annotation, showing that certain cell-type-specific SVGs given by MMM may contribute to cell–cell communications and regulate the tissue microenvironment. Furthermore, we applied MMM to the 3D tissue models constructed from multiple ST slices, demonstrating its utility in 3D data analysis.   Results   Method Overview.  Given the common presence of cell type mixtures across space in the spatially resolved transcriptomic data, identifying cell-type-specific SVGs requires not only separating the contributions of different cell types from overall gene expression measurements but also inferring whether each contribution varies across spatial locations. This appears to be a challenging task; however, with the rapid advancement of cell type deconvolution methods ( 26 ,  27 ,  33 ,  34 ), their reliable performance offers opportunities to explore cell-type-specific signals. Hence, we propose a probabilistic framework called MMM that enables the statistical characterization of cell-type-specific SVGs by accounting for the cell type mixtures in the spatial context.  Specifically, we begin the model design with raw count data rather than transformed data to avoid information loss. Then, we use a log-mixture structure to account for cell type composition while simultaneously correcting for the spot and platform effects between ST and scRNA-seq data. To characterize the gene expression profile of each cell type at a spot, we decompose it on the log scale into three components: the average gene expression of the certain cell type, spatial variations, and nonspatial variations.  To handle the above complicated model, we propose a powerful score testing framework that can evaluate the significance of cell-type-specific spatial variations. We first develop an approximate algorithm based on penalized quasi-likelihood (PQL) ( 35 ,  36 ) to fit the null model efficiently. Then, we construct a cell-type-specific test statistic in quadratic form ( 28 ) to examine the significance of the variance component related to spatial locations. This test statistic incorporates a cell-type-specific membership matrix to account for the contributions from individual cell types, effectively addressing the confounding issues arising from the cell type mixtures in the ST data. Since the true expression patterns of genes are largely unknown and can vary across genes and cell types in practice, we use the Cauchy combination method ( 37 ,  38 ) to aggregate the information from diverse spatial kernels that cover a wide spectrum of common spatial patterns observed in real datasets. The technical details are provided in  Materials and Methods , and   SI Appendix ,  Methods  .    Simulation Study.  We first conducted a comprehensive simulation study to benchmark MMM and related methods ( 15 ,  23 – 25 ,  31 ). To mimic the cell type mixtures in the ST data, we simulated two cell types, termed cell type 1 and cell type 2, which are colocalized at each spot ( Fig. 1 A  ). Simulated gene expression data were generated with various cell-type-specific spatial patterns, including “null” (randomly distributed), “gradient” (change along the    x   -axis), “hotspot” (highly expressed in the central circular region), and “streak” (highly expressed in the middle streak region) ( Fig. 1 B  ). For each experiment, we simulated 2,000 genes, where all of them were null genes for both cell types in the null case, while 10 of them were SVGs specific to cell type 1, and the rest were null genes for both cell types in the alternative case. Details are provided in   SI Appendix ,  Simulation Design  .    Fig. 1.   Simulation study to benchmark MMM and related methods. ( A ) Schematic diagram of cell type mixtures with the two colors representing the proportions of cell types 1 and 2 in each spot. ( B ) Simulated cell-type-specific gene expression patterns. The color indicates the normalized expression level compared to the mean from scRNA-seq reference data, corresponding to     u   i  ,  k  ,  g      in the MMM model. Pink represents higher expression      u   i  ,  k  ,  g    >  0    , while green indicates lower expression      u   i  ,  k  ,  g    <  0    . ( C ) Spatial distributions of the two cell types. ( D  and  E ) Quantile-quantile plots of the observed     −   log  10    (  p  )      against the expected     −   log  10    (  p  )     . ( F ) Spatial distributions of the two cell types. ( G  and  H ) Quantile-quantile plots of the observed     −   log  10    (  p  )      against the expected     −   log  10    (  p  )     . Spatial distribution of normalized spot effects      α  ^   i    ’s estimated by RCTD in Scenario 3. ( J ) Histogram of platform effects      γ  ^   g    ’s of all genes estimated by RCTD in Scenario 3. ( K ) Quantile-quantile plot of the observed     −   log  10    (  p  )      against the expected     −   log  10    (  p  )     .( L ) Comparison of statistical power in the alternative case, with each panel corresponding to a spatial pattern depicted in ( B ). ( M ) Density functions of varied prior distributions considered in Scenario 5. The dashed lines represent the density functions of the Gaussian distributions with the same variances. ( N  and  O ) Quantile-quantile plots of the observed     −   log  10    (  p  )      against the expected     −   log  10    (  p  )     .                   To highlight the essential role of each component in the MMM model, we simulated five scenarios, where Scenarios 1 and 2 were designed to study the influence of cell type mixtures on SVG identification, Scenario 3 was designed to study the spot and platform effects, Scenario 4 combined all the above settings to mimic the real ST data, and Scenario 5 was designed to study the method’s sensitivity to prior assumptions. Specifically, in Scenario 1, the proportions of cell types 1 and 2 were uniformly distributed across space ( Fig. 1 C  ). We found that the    P   -values of MMM and non-cell-type-specific methods were well calibrated in the null case ( Fig. 1 D  ), and all methods could successfully identify SVGs in the alternative case; however, non-cell-type-specific methods could not clarify which cell type the spatial variations belonged to ( Fig. 1 E  ). In Scenario 2, we varied the cell type composition spatially, with the proportion of cell type 1 increasing from left to right and cell type 2 in contrast ( Fig. 1 F  ). Due to the heterogeneity in gene expression profiles between the two cell types, non-cell-type-specific methods identified their respective marker genes as SVGs ( Fig. 1 H  ), while MMM controlled type I errors by accounting for the cell type composition ( Fig. 1 G  ). In Scenario 3, we kept the cell type composition randomly distributed, but varied the spot effects     α  i    ’s spatially ( Fig. 1 I  ) and added gene-specific platform effects     γ  g    ’s ( Fig. 1 J  ). This was designed to examine the robustness of the methods in the presence of batch effects and platform differences. Under the null condition, MMM still produced well-calibrated    P   -values for both cell types by explicitly correcting for these two effects, while Celina yielded slightly inflated results ( Fig. 1 K  ). Celina’s unsatisfactory control of type I errors in this scenario is likely due to its modeling of normalized data instead of raw count data. The data transformation step before analysis can lead to an inadequate characterization of the mean–variance relationship inherent in raw count data and introduce systematic errors ( 23 ,  39 ), thus preventing the full elimination of confounding bias. In Scenario 4, we combined the settings of Scenarios 2 and 3 so that the cell type composition varied spatially, as well as varying spot effects and gene-specific platform effects. We should note that Scenario 4 was the most challenging and also the closest to real ST data. We then set the false discovery rate cutoff at 0.05 and measured the power of MMM and other cell-type-specific methods in the alternative case ( Fig. 1 L  ). We found that MMM was more powerful than the other two methods across all settings of spatial pattern and sample size because of its ability to aggregate information from multiple kernels corresponding to diverse spatial patterns and correction for spot effects and platform effects. Compared to Celina, the power gain of MMM is due to its modeling of raw count data, which avoids the loss of information ( 23 ,  40 ). C-SIDE suffered from low statistical power since its nonparametric mode is not effective in combining the    P   -values corresponding to multiple basis functions (  SI Appendix ,  Methods  ). However, we should emphasize that its performance here does not reflect the effectiveness in the task for which it was originally designed. In Scenario 5, we generated data with varied prior distributions, including “flat top,” “skew,” and “bimodal” ( Fig. 1 M  ), to evaluate whether MMM is sensitive to prior assumptions (more prior settings in   SI Appendix , Fig. S1 ). We examined MMM under the null condition first ( Fig. 1 N  ) and then in the alternative case ( Fig. 1 O  ). The obtained    P   -values were well calibrated in all settings, showing that MMM is robust to prior assumptions. In summary, MMM outperformed all existing methods in various scenarios, both in terms of statistical power and type I error control.    MMM Distinguishes Cell-Type-Specific SVGs from Cell Type Marker Genes in the 10x Visium Human Dorsolateral Prefrontal Cortex Dataset.  Here, we evaluated our method on a human dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) dataset generated by 10x Visium ( 41 ), which consists of two pairs of adjacent slices with indices 151673–151676 from a neurotypical adult donor. Throughout the analysis of the four Visium slices, a single-nucleus RNA-sequencing (snRNA-seq) dataset of human DLPFC ( 42 ) was used as reference.  By comparing with the methods that do not consider cell type composition, we demonstrated MMM’s ability to distinguish cell-type-specific SVGs from cell type marker genes. We began with a single slice with index 151673 and used STitch3D ( 34 ) to estimate cell type proportions (     β  ^    i  ,  k     ’s;  Fig. 2 A  ), spot effects (     α  ^   i    ’s;  Fig. 2 B  ), and platform effects (     γ  ^   g    ’s;  Fig. 2 C  ). Clearly, there existed layer-specific spot effects and heterogeneous gene platform effects, and ignoring these effects can lead to confounded results when identifying cell-type-specific SVGs. To highlight the differences between MMM and non-cell-type-specific methods, we applied MMM alongside SPARK, SPARK-X, and HEARTSVG. Comparing the obtained    P   -values, genes  MAG ,  KRT17 , and  HS6ST3  were flagged as SVGs by SPARK, SPARK-X, and HEARTSVG ( Fig. 2 D  , first row), while MMM’s results indicated that these genes were not cell-type-specific SVGs ( Fig. 2 D  , second row). Since these three genes were the marker genes of Oligos_3, Ex_8_L5_6, and Ex_10_L2_4, respectively, their spatial expression patterns were mainly affected by the distribution of cell type proportions across space ( Fig. 2 E  , first and second rows). In contrast, MMM successfully revealed that their spatial variations were evenly distributed within a specific cell type by accounting for the cell type composition ( Fig. 2 E  , last row), as indicated by the maximum a posteriori estimate of the variations      u  ^    i  ,  k  ,  g      compared to the logarithm of mean expression (  SI Appendix ,  Methods  ). MMM Produces Replicable Results Across Multiple Adult Mouse Brain Datasets and Reveals the Heritability Enrichment in Brain-Related Phenotypes.  In this section, we applied MMM to multiple adult mouse brain ST datasets, including two slices profiled by the 10x Visium platform ( 43 ) and a 3D model constructed using 35 slices profiled by Spatial Transcriptomics ( 44 ). By comparing the results from several datasets, we showed MMM’s robustness and consistency across different sources and perspectives. We further showed that the heritability of cell-type-specific SVGs was significantly enriched in certain brain-related traits and diseases. Throughout the study, we used STitch3D to perform cell type deconvolution with a mouse brain snRNA-seq dataset containing 59 cell types ( 27 ) as reference.  We first focused on the two Visium slices of the adult mouse brain across the antero-posterior (AP) axis ( 43 ), which have relatively well-defined morphological characteristics and thus allow a reasonable and distinct comparison ( Fig. 3 A  ). For example, the thalamus is a paired structure of gray matter situated near the brain center, and the hippocampus exhibits consistent thickness and comprises curve-shaped subregions, including three cornu ammonis (CA) areas and the dentate gyrus (DG). Compared to existing methods, the    P   -values obtained from MMM were more consistent between two slices ( Fig. 3 B   and   SI Appendix , Fig. S6 ). Then, we focused on four cell-type-specific SVGs identified by MMM, including Ext_Thal_1-specific SVGs  Pcp4  and  Nrxn3  ( Fig. 3 C  ), and Inh_1-specific SVGs  Calb1  and  Gnas  ( Fig. 3 E  ). We found that their expression variations revealed by MMM within respective cell types showed replicable spatial patterns between the two slices ( Fig. 3  D  and  F  ). Next, we applied MMM to a 3D model constructed from 35 coronal slices of a mouse brain hemisphere along the AP axis. The original dataset was generated by Spatial Transcriptomics technology ( 44 ). A significant challenge in analyzing the 3D model was the presence of batch effects across the numerous slices. However, the cell-type-specific SVGs identified by MMM exhibited spatial patterns that closely aligned with previous findings ( Fig. 3  G  and  H  ). This suggests that MMM is effective in correcting batch effects across the slices. To further demonstrate the robustness of MMM, we evaluated its sensitivity to different deconvolution methods and rotation of spatial coordinates. First, by integrating MMM with estimated cell type proportions by RCTD and STitch3D, respectively, we found that it produced consistent results for most cell types (  SI Appendix , Fig. S7 ). Then, we applied MMM to the two slices rotated by 45 degrees and compared the results before and after rotation, showing its rotational invariance (  SI Appendix , Fig. S8 ). Overall, the cell-type-specific SVG identification results from MMM were stable and reliable in practice.    Fig. 3. In many recent studies, the integration of the risk genetic variants identified by GWASs with other omics data provides valuable insights into mechanisms that underlie complex traits and diseases ( 16 ,  46 – 53 ). For example, the integration of GWAS and scRNA-seq data is a promising way to reveal functional programs and pathogenic processes at the single-cell resolution ( 49 – 52 ). However, the heterogeneity within the same cell type and lack of spatial location information severely limit accurate investigations of cellular origins in the tissue context. Although some methods attempt to utilize the ST data to tackle the above challenges ( 16 ), the common cell type mixtures confound the results and prevent finer resolution analyses. Here, we conducted an integrative analysis of the GWAS summary statistics data from European ancestry with the cell-type-specific SVGs identified by MMM. Unlike the heritability enrichment analysis for highly expressed genes in certain tissues, our analysis provides a unique angle to characterize risk genes within a spatially variable and cell-type-specific context.  To do so, we aligned mouse genes with human genes, which is a commonly adopted strategy for studying human traits and diseases ( 16 ,  46 ,  50 ). Despite the biological differences between humans and mice, arguments in favor of using mouse RNA-seq data have been well discussed ( 50 ). First, gene expression profiles are highly conserved between humans and mice ( 54 ), which is supported by a series of large-scale projects ( 55 – 57 ). Second, high-quality RNA-seq data are more readily obtainable from mice than from humans. Third, the key findings from mouse data have been successfully replicated in human data in previous studies ( 50 ). After aligning mouse genes with human genes, we mapped these genes to single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the human genome based on reference annotations ( 58 ), and generated a unique set of SNP annotations for each cell type. By treating a cell-type-specific SNP set as the annotation set, we evaluated whether the corresponding cell-type-specific genes can explain a higher proportion of heritability for a phenotype using the stratified linkage disequilibrium score regression (S-LDSC) ( 46 ,  59 ,  60 ). Here, we considered GWAS summary statistics data for 30 phenotypes (  SI Appendix , Table S1 ), which can be roughly grouped into two categories: brain-related, including intelligence (IQ), memory, reaction time (RT), verbal-numerical reasoning (VNR), Alzheimer’s disease (AD), epilepsy, Parkinson’s disease (PD), anorexia nervosa (AN), attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), autism spectrum disorder (ASD), bipolar disorder (BD), cannabis use disorder (CUD), depression, insomnia, obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), obsessive-compulsive symptoms (OCS), posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), schizophrenia (SCZ), and Tourette syndrome (TS); and non-brain-related, including coronary artery disease (CAD), high blood pressure (HBP), pulse rate (PR), free thyroxine (FT4), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), type 2 diabetes (T2D), body mass index (BMI), waist-hip ratio (WHR), bone mineral density (BMD), height, and osteoarthritis (OA).  Note that we have two groups of genes for heritability enrichment analysis: cell-type-specific highly expressed genes identified from scRNA-seq reference data and cell-type-specific SVGs identified by MMM in ST data (  SI Appendix , Table S3 ). To differentiate the contributions of two groups of genes, we first examined the heritability enrichment of genes that are highly expressed in each brain cell type. After mapping these brain-expressed genes to the human genome, we conducted S-LDSC using its baseline model, which includes 52 functional categories (such as genic regions, enhancer regions, and conserved regions) along with a category encompassing all SNPs. For each cell type, we incorporated the SNP annotation set derived from cell-type-specific highly expressed genes into the baseline model and estimated their heritability enrichment across the 30 phenotypes. We summarized the results of the enrichment analysis in   SI Appendix , Fig. S9  and observed significant enrichment of these highly expressed genes in most brain-related phenotypes. In contrast, no cell types in this mouse brain study exhibited statistically significant enrichment in non-brain-related traits or diseases, serving as a negative control. To characterize the contribution from cell-type-specific SVGs, we further added an SNP annotation set derived from the cell-type-specific SVGs to the baseline model of S-LDSC, conditional on the cell-type-specific highly expressed gene sets. The results of the heritability enrichment analysis are shown in  Fig. 3 I  . We found that the SVGs of certain neuronal subtypes were significantly enriched in cognitive traits, such as IQ and VNR, as well as in psychiatric disorders, including BD, depression, insomnia, OCD, and TS. This indicates that within these neuronal subtypes, only a subset of cells in specific regions drives the corresponding traits and diseases by regulating the expression of genetics-informed genes. For example,  Nrxn3  was an SVG within Ext_Thal_1 cells ( Fig. 3 D  ) while its human homolog  NRXN3  links synaptic cell adhesion to cognition and its disorders ( 61 – 63 ). Mutations in  NRXN3  may cause developmental delay, learning disability, movement disorder, and behavioral problems ( 64 ,  65 ). We further performed a gene analysis using MAGMA ( 66 ) with GWAS summary statistics data as input and found that  NRXN3  was significantly associated with IQ (adjusted    P   -value     1.284  ×   10   −  4      ). In summary, the cell-type-specific SVGs identified by MMM have great potential to elucidate the mechanisms underlying complex traits and diseases in the spatial context through integrative analysis with GWAS hits.    MMM Yields Robust Results on the 10x Xenium Human Breast Cancer Dataset by Accounting for the Uncertainties in Cell Segmentation and Annotation.  To demonstrate the effectiveness and stability of our method for high-resolution ST data, we applied MMM to a human breast cancer dataset ( 7 ) generated by 10x Xenium, a recently developed imaging-based ST technology that enables measurement of gene expression at the subcellular resolution. The reference data were generated using Chromium Single Cell Gene Expression Flex applied to FFPE tissues (scFFPE-seq) in the same study ( 7 ).  Typically, analyzing the Xenium data at the single-cell level requires two preprocessing steps: cell segmentation ( 67 – 69 ) and cell type annotation ( 70 ,  71 ) ( Fig. 4 A  ), both of which introduce significant uncertainties in the subsequent investigations. For example, in the cell segmentation step, imperfect boundary division can lead to the inclusion of transcripts from surrounding cells into the target cell, thus causing cell mixtures. More importantly, these transcripts may not belong to the target cell type ( Fig. 4 B  ). Second, cell type annotation in a deterministic way can also bring uncertainty due to imperfect segmentation. To demonstrate this, we applied RCTD to estimate the probabilities of the most likely cell type of each cell segmented by UCS ( 69 ). Notably, many cells exhibit a probability of less than 0.75 for their most likely cell type ( Fig. 4 C  ). Ignoring these uncertainties can lead to inflated type I error rates when identifying cell-type-specific SVGs. Moreover, outcomes from different segmentation and annotation methods can vary a lot ( Fig. 4 D  ), posing a huge challenge for reproducibility. Within the MMM framework, we use the log-mixture structure to account for these uncertainties in the probabilistic view, producing consistent    P   -values when using segmented cells from different methods as input ( Fig. 4 E  , first row); in contrast, ignoring these uncertainties may lead to substantially less consistent results ( Fig. 4 E  , second row). We also visualized the expression levels of cell-type-specific SVGs identified by MMM ( Fig. 4 F  ), some of which exhibited distinct expression patterns across different spatial domains, suggesting that these spatial variations were driven by the activities of other cells in niches with high probability. For example, the expression levels of  CEACAM6  within DCIS 2 cells were significantly different between the area surrounded by invasive tumor cells and other areas ( Fig. 4 G  ). To further verify this conjecture, we performed spatial clustering to assign cells to clusters based on their niches using CellCharter ( 72 ) ( Fig. 4 H  ). We found that DCIS 2 cells distributed in different areas were assigned to different clusters (blue and pink), indicating the differences in their microenvironment structures. We then applied niche-DE ( 19 ) to perform the niche-differential expression analysis. We noted that the identified cell type DCIS 2-specific niche-associated genes, which were differentially expressed within DCIS 2 cells in the context of specific spatial niches, had a considerable degree of overlap with the SVG results from MMM (  SI Appendix , Fig. S10 ). For example,  CEACAM6  was a niche-associated gene of DCIS 2 cells regulated by the presence of invasive tumor cells with adjusted    P   -value     5.550  ×   10   −  3       reported by niche-DE, suggesting certain signaling molecule interactions. This was also validated by applying CellChat ( 73 ), whose results revealed that  CEACAM  signaling pathway was an important component of the cell–cell communication network in this breast cancer dataset (  SI Appendix , Fig. S10 ). Notably,  CEACAM6  is a cell surface receptor that can influence intracellular signaling events and plays an important role in tumorigenesis, invasion, and metastasis in vivo ( 74 – 77 ). It was also found that  CEACAM6  could potentially contribute to the regulation in the tumor-stroma boundary, which was correlated with the efficacy of immunotherapy ( 78 ). Further investigations, such as  CEACAM6 -targeted antibody–drug conjugate, has shown promising improvement in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma therapy ( 79 ). Finally, we used g:Profiler ( 80 ) to perform a functional enrichment analysis for the DCIS 2 cell-type-specific SVG set (  SI Appendix , Fig. S11 ) and found that it was enriched in gene ontology (GO) terms including anchoring junction (adjusted    P   -value     7.621  ×   10   −  6      ), apical junction complex (adjusted    P   -value     8.208  ×   10   −  6      ), cell–cell junction (adjusted    P   -value     2.957  ×   10   −  4      ), apoptotic process (adjusted    P   -value     8.942  ×   10   −  4      ), and cell adhesion (adjusted    P   -value     2.604  ×   10   −  3      ).    MMM Provides Consistent Results Across Multiple Colorectal Cancer Datasets from Different ST Technologies.  To evaluate whether MMM can obtain consistent results between ST data with varied resolutions, we applied MMM to the human colorectal cancer (CRC) datasets ( 8 ) generated by multiple ST technologies, including Visium, Visium HD, and Xenium. Throughout the study, we used the paired scRNA-seq dataset provided in the same study ( 8 ) as reference. We chose the CRC data for two reasons: First, the extreme sparsity and high noise level in the Visium HD data make the analysis particularly challenging, allowing us to highlight MMM’s advantage in statistical power; Second, the paired Visium and Xenium data could be used for verifying the identified cell-type-specific SVGs in the absence of ground truth.  In the tumor boundary region, cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are often the most abundant stromal cell type, and macrophages are the most abundant immune cell type, both playing crucial roles in shaping the tumor microenvironment (TME). Therefore, we focused on CAFs and macrophages to make the comparison between MMM and existing methods. Since there was no ground truth on cell-type-specific SVGs, we first applied these methods to the Visium HD data and then used the identified cell-type-specific SVGs in the paired Visium data for verification. In the comparison with Celina and C-SIDE, we found that MMM not only identified more cell-type-specific SVGs but also achieved greater consistency between the Visium HD and Visium data (  SI Appendix , Figs. S12 and S13 ). This showed that MMM enjoys higher statistical power when handling sparse and noisy data and thus could obtain more reliable results. We also visualized the spatial patterns of the identified SVGs for CAFs and macrophages using the datasets generated by Visium, Visium HD, and Xenium ( Fig. 5 A  ). We found that the spatial patterns revealed by MMM were highly consistent between different ST technologies. Specifically, MMM identified the colocalization of two subpopulations,  MMP11  +  CAFs and  SPP1  +  macrophages, around the tumor boundary region, providing insights into the tumor microenvironment. High-resolution data have unique advantages when analyzing rare cell types. For example, CD4 +  T cells and CD8 +  cytotoxic T cells are sparsely distributed across the tissue, resulting in very low cell type proportions in the most Visium spots (  SI Appendix , Fig. S14 ). This low abundance poses significant challenges for the cell-type-specific SVG analysis, as the target cell types can easily be confounded by other cell types in the spots. However, in high-resolution data, such as the Visium HD data, a considerable number of spots exhibit high proportions of these rare cell types, making it feasible to identify SVGs for them and obtain reliable results. Specifically, we applied MMM to the paired Visium HD and Xenium data with a focus on CD4 +  T cells and CD8 +  cytotoxic T cells ( Fig. 5 B  ). The spatial patterns of the identified cell-type-specific SVGs by MMM still showed high consistency between the two technologies. MMM Reveals 3D Spatial Patterns of the Cell-Type-Specific SVGs in the Drosophila Embryo Model Constructed from the Stereo-Seq Dataset.  In this section, we used MMM to analyze a 16 to 18 h  Drosophila  embryo dataset within certain spatial domains to present more distinct spatial patterns of the identified cell-type-specific SVGs in the organ context. The dataset comprises multiple Stereo-seq ST slices ( 81 ), where spatial bins were created by aggregating     20  ×  20     DNA nanoballs into 10 µm    ×    10 µm-sized bins as described in the original study. The reference data were built upon a three-level single-cell combinatorial-indexing RNA-sequencing (sci-RNA-seq3)  Drosophila  embryo atlas ( 82 ) that matches the same developmental stage as the ST data.  We began by applying STitch3D to estimate cell type proportions and reconstruct a 3D whole organism spatial atlas based on 13 ST slices. STitch3D successfully reconstructed the central nervous system (CNS) and salivary gland, which fully recapitulated the morphology and position of the corresponding organ structures ( Fig. 6  A  and  C  ). We then zoomed in on these two specific regions and applied MMM to reveal the spatial patterns of cell-type-specific SVGs in the organ context. Compared to other tasks in which MMM was examined, the detection of cell-type-specific SVGs for the  Drosophila  embryo faced additional challenges. First, the Stereo-seq dataset is highly sparse with more than 95% genes in most spatial bins having zero counts. Second, only a small subset of spots are available for analysis when we focus on a specific region. Both data sparsity and the limited sample size severely restrict statistical power. Moreover, batch differences among multiple ST slices largely confound the results and may cause false discoveries ( Fig. 6  B  and  D   and   SI Appendix , Fig. S15 ). Despite the difficulties, MMM successfully identified cell-type-specific SVGs and enjoyed high statistical power by directly working on raw count data rather than transformed data while simultaneously correcting for slice-specific effects (  SI Appendix ,  Methods  ). We further visualized the expression variations of the identified cell-type-specific SVGs and found that these genes varied significantly at different locations within certain organs ( Fig. 6  B  and  D  ), suggesting that they may correspond to different developmental states or functions. For example,  jim  is an important transcription factor in neuronal diversity ( 83 ) and elimination ( 84 ), and  fabp  overexpression enhances autophagy and suppresses amyloid- pathology, leading to potential therapeutic interventions for Alzheimer’s disease ( 85 ). We also used g:Profiler ( 80 ) to perform a functional enrichment analysis (  SI Appendix , Fig. S16 ). In the analysis of the CNS-specific SVGs, we noted that the GO terms related to CNS development were significantly enriched, such as neuron differentiation (adjusted    P   -value     2.050  ×   10   −  4      ) and generation (adjusted    P   -value     4.979  ×   10   −  4      ). Discussion  Characterizing spatial variations in gene expression with cell type specificity is an important but challenging task for investigating the landscape of complex biological systems in ST studies. In this paper, we have presented MMM, a unified statistical framework for identifying cell-type-specific SVGs in ST datasets. The success of MMM relies on its three carefully designed features. First, MMM uses a log-mixture structure to decompose the measured gene expression at each spot into a linear combination of fractions attributed to different cell types on the log scale, so that the cell-type-specific SVG identification results will not be dominated by the marker genes of certain cell types. Second, MMM explicitly corrects for platform differences between ST and scRNA-seq data, which are commonly found in real data. In contrast, overlooking this difference can lead to misidentification of spatial variations. Third, MMM can aggregate the information across multiple kernels corresponding to various spatial patterns. Therefore, the overall assessment output by MMM is effective in detecting unknown and potentially diverse spatial patterns in real ST data. Through comprehensive numerical experiments, we showed that MMM enjoys high statistical power while appropriately controlling type I error. We also used MMM to analyze eight publicly available ST datasets of different tissues generated from various platforms with a wide range of resolutions. The experimental results indicate that even for high-resolution ST data, considering cell type mixtures contributes to obtaining more stable, reliable, and replicable results. MMM is also easily applicable to 3D datasets, providing more biological insights from the 3D perspective.  MMM directly models the raw RNA counts to avoid information loss, resulting in an appreciable power gain ( 23 ,  40 ). In contrast, ignoring the count nature of ST data can lead to an inadequate characterization of the mean–variance relationship and introduce systematic errors ( 23 ,  39 ). Since the normal distribution cannot approximate the sparse data appropriately, such a power gain is apparent in data with low counts, especially for the recently developed high-resolution ST technologies like Stereo-seq and Visium HD. However, when handling data with high counts, the Gaussian approximation approach is attractive since a more computationally efficient algorithm can be derived.  Finally, we discuss the limitations and potential extensions of MMM. First, MMM requires annotated scRNA-seq data for reference-based deconvolution of cell types. The quality of scRNA-seq reference data and how well it matches the ST data have a notable influence on the deconvolution results and, subsequently, on the cell-type-specific SVG identification. Although single-cell atlases are increasingly available, they may contain missing cell types or substantial platform effects. More importantly, some ST tissues may lack the corresponding scRNA-seq reference data. Second, the cell types of interest may be present in extremely low quantities, which can make MMM unreliable for such rare cell types due to limited sample sizes and significant confounding effects from other cell types mixed together. These limitations may not be reflected in simulations because of the idealized settings of cell type composition but have essential impacts in practice as shown in our analysis of the Visium adult mouse brain dataset (  SI Appendix , Fig. S7 ). Third, while our nonparametric statistical testing framework makes MMM powerful in characterizing general spatial gene expression patterns, their clear biological interpretations still rely on specific scientific questions and further investigations, as illustrated in our real data analyses. Compared to the methods that work on the predefined covariates, such as C-SIDE and niche-DE (  SI Appendix ,  Methods  ), MMM is more suitable for preliminary exploration in cases where prior understanding of ST data is limited. Fourth, MMM was designed to capture spatially correlated cellular heterogeneity by considering each gene separately. However, combining all genes together could provide gene-set-level information. For example, representing the variations of spots across all genes through the low-rank approximation ( 86 – 89 ) generates interpretable factors that can provide deeper biological insights, such as the identification of metagenes, and gene–gene covariation and coregulation analysis.    Materials and Methods   The MMM Method.  Let     Y  =    Y   i  ,  g     ∈    N    I  ×  G       denote the observed gene expression count matrix with the spot index     i  ∈   1  ,  ⋯  ,  I      and the gene index     g  ∈   1  ,  ⋯  ,  G     . To account for cell type mixtures, we first need to determine the cell type proportions       β   i   =     β   i  ,  1    ,  ⋯  ,   β   i  ,  K      T    ∈    R   K      of each spot    i   , where    K    represents the total number of cell types. This can be estimated using deconvolution methods ( 26 ,  34 ) with the paired scRNA-seq data as reference. We further introduce two effects, denoted as      α   =    α  i    ∈    R   I      and      γ  g   ∈  R    , to account for spot-specific effects and gene-specific platform effects between ST and scRNA-seq data. Specifically,     α     captures the variations in total transcript counts between spots while     γ  g     accounts for the expression differences of gene    g    between scRNA-seq and ST platforms. As we are interested in cell-type-specific spatial gene expression patterns, we next introduce the cell-type-specific gene expression profiles       η    i  ,  g    =     η   i  ,  1  ,  g    ,  ⋯  ,   η   i  ,  K  ,  g      T    ∈    R   K      for spot    i   . Then, we consider the following hierarchical probabilistic model ( 26 ,  90 ) for the observed RNA counts in ST data:   [1]         Y   i  ,  g    ∣   λ   i  ,  g        ∼   Poisson    N  i    λ   i  ,  g     ,          [2]        log    λ   i  ,  g         =   x  i   T      ξ   g   +  log     ∑   k  =  1   K     β   i  ,  k     η   i  ,  k  ,  g     +   α  i   +   γ  g   ,         where     N  i     is the total transcript count for spot    i   ,     λ   i  ,  g      represents the underlying expression level of gene    g    for spot    i   ,      x  i   =     x   i  ,  1    ,  ⋯  ,   x   i  ,  C      T    ∈    R   C      is the covariate vector, and       ξ   g   =     ξ   1  ,  g    ,  ⋯  ,   ξ   C  ,  g      T    ∈    R   C      is the vector of corresponding fixed effects shared by all spots. The log-mixture structure (Eq.   2  ) separates the contributions of different cell types by representing     λ   i  ,  g      as the weighted sum of    K    cell-type-specific gene expressions     η   i  ,  k  ,  g     ’s after adjusting for the multiplicative effects      x  i   T      ξ   g     ,     α  i    , and     γ  g    . To characterize cell-type-specific gene expression profiles, we decompose     η   i  ,  k  ,  g      into three components ( 15 ,  23 ) on its log scale as   [3]        log   η   i  ,  k  ,  g    =     log   η   0  ,  k  ,  g     ⏟    log mean expression    +     b   i  ,  k  ,  g    ⏟    spatial variation    +     ϵ   i  ,  k  ,  g    ⏟    nonspatial variation    ,         where     log   η   0  ,  k  ,  g       is the logarithm of mean expression of cell type    k   , calculated from scRNA-seq data. The term     b   i  ,  k  ,  g      captures the cell-type-specific spatial correlation pattern among spots at different locations, and     ϵ   i  ,  k  ,  g      represents nonspatial variation. Given the mixed-effects model (Eq.   3  ), we now focus on how to distinguish the spatial variation term from the nonspatial variation term. For gene    g   , we use a stationary Gaussian process to characterize the spatial variations from the same cell type    k    across all spots and an independent and identical Gaussian prior for all nonspatial variations ( 23 )   [4]           b   1  ,  k  ,  g    ,  ⋯  ,   b   I  ,  k  ,  g      T    ∼  N   0  ,   τ   k  ,  g    K   ,    ϵ   i  ,  k  ,  g    ∼  N   0  ,   τ   ϵ  ,  g     ,         where matrix     K  ∈    R    I  ×  I       is a prespecified kernel function of the spatial locations      ℓ   =      ℓ   1   ,  ⋯  ,    ℓ   I     T       with the     (  i  ,   i  ′   )    -th element being      K   i  ,   i  ′     =  K     ℓ   i   ,    ℓ    i  ′       ,     τ   k  ,  g      is the scaling factor of spatial variance component, and     τ   ϵ  ,  g      is the variance of nonspatial variance component. In contrast to existing methods, our formulation presents several unique advantages for characterizing spatial gene expression patterns in a cell-type-specific manner. First, we begin the model design with raw count data rather than transformed data to avoid information loss. Second, the log-mixture structure (Eq.   2  ) accounts for cell type composition while simultaneously correcting for the spot and platform effects between ST and scRNA-seq data. Third, the mixed-effects model (Eq.   3  ) enables a statistically rigorous way to examine the significance of spatial variations. Furthermore, our model can be extended to identify cell-type-specific SVGs in the multiple slices case and allow for batch effect correction by modeling the slice-specific effects (  SI Appendix ,  Methods  ). In summary, these features enable MMM to seamlessly integrate with the count-based deconvolution methods, establishing it as a unified framework for identifying cell-type-specific SVGs.  To determine whether gene    g    is an SVG of cell type    k   , we can formulate it as a statistical testing problem with the null hypothesis      H  0   :   τ   k  ,  g    =  0     against the alternative one      H  1   :   τ   k  ,  g    >  0    . To evaluate the significance of the spatial variance component of each cell type, we propose a powerful score testing framework while considering the cell type mixtures. Specifically, we first develop an approximate algorithm based on PQL ( 35 ,  36 ) to fit the null model efficiently. After the algorithm converges, we obtain the pseudo data vector       Y  ~   g   ∈    R   I      in the linear mixed-effects form with the marginal distribution       Y  ~   g   ∼  N   X    ξ   g   ,    Σ   g       under the null condition. Here,     X  =     x  1   ,  ⋯  ,   x  I     T    ∈    R    I  ×  C       is the covariate matrix of all spots,       Σ   g   =   W  g   −  1    +   ∑   k  =  1   K    M   k  ,  g      τ   ϵ  ,  g    I    M   k  ,  g    T       is the covariance matrix,      W  g   ∈    R    I  ×  I       is the diagonal weight matrix, and      M   k  ,  g    ∈    R    I  ×  I       is the membership matrix indicating the contribution of cell type    k    to each spot. Motivated by the marginal distribution of pseudo data, we construct a cell-type-specific test statistic in quadratic form ( 28 )   [5]         Q   k  ,  g    =   1  2      P  g     Y  ~   g     T      M   k  ,  g    K   M   k  ,  g    T       P  g     Y  ~   g    ,         which follows a mixture of     χ  1  2     distributions under the null hypothesis. Here,     P  g     is the symmetric projection matrix defined as      P  g   =    Σ   g   −  1    −    Σ   g   −  1    X     X   T      Σ   g   −  1    X    −  1     X   T      Σ   g   −  1      . Note that     M   k  ,  g      contains cell-type-specific information to help address the confounding issue due to the mixture of cell types. The power of the above hypothesis testing scheme is obviously determined by how well the kernel matrix    K    matches the true spatial variations exhibited by the gene of interest within a certain cell type. Hence, we use the Cauchy combination method ( 38 ) to aggregate information from multiple kernels capturing various spatial patterns and calculate a calibrated    P   -value as the overall assessment. The technical details are provided in   SI Appendix ,  Methods  .    Seamless Integration with Cell Type Deconvolution.  Through our numerical experiments, we found that the cell type proportions estimated by the deconvolution methods play an important role in the cell-type-specific SVG identification, and thus keeping the deconvolution and SVG models consistent in terms of the definition of cell type proportion is essential to controlling the false positive rates and yielding reliable and interpretable results. Within the MMM model, cell type proportion     β   i  ,  k      represents the ratio of the transcript count from cell type    k    to all transcripts at spot    i   . Here, we highlight its difference with another definition based on the number of cells rather than transcripts. Therefore, we chose RCTD ( 26 ) and STitch3D ( 34 ) to estimate the cell type proportions for MMM throughout the study. Specifically, we used RCTD for the single ST slice case and STitch3D for the multiple ST slices case. For a fair comparison in simulation studies, we used RCTD for C-SIDE ( 15 ) and CARD ( 33 ) for Celina ( 31 ) as suggested in their tutorial websites. The above deconvolution methods all require the ST data with paired scRNA-seq data as input.  MMM offers a seamless integration with the results from the deconvolution step. For example, the cell type proportion     β   i  ,  k     , spot-specific effect     α  i    , and gene-specific platform effect     γ  g     can be directly inherited from the cell type deconvolution step. After plugging in these estimates, no identifiable issue exists when fitting the MMM model. While the first two are necessary (    β   i  ,  k      and     α  i    ), users can choose not to provide the platform effect     γ  g    , and our software would estimate it automatically by adding a column of ones in the covariate matrix    X    and treating     γ  g     as an intercept term. Comparing to fixing     γ  g     the same as the deconvolution result, we observed better model fits by leaving it unconstrained in practice. The reason can be that the deconvolution model may not be exactly the same as the SVG model, and the latter fitted one should therefore be more appropriate. As a result, we allow     γ  g     to be a free parameter in our software.    Computational Efficiency.  We analyze the computational complexity of the MMM method to assess its scalability. Given the RNA count matrix     Y  ∈    N    I  ×  G       containing    I    sample spots and    G    genes, we focus on one gene at a time since all genes can be processed in parallel in our software implementation. To fit the null model, the MMM algorithm requires     O    I  2       computations. In the statistical testing step, we need     O    I  2       computations to evaluate the test statistics. Therefore, the overall computations are quadratic to the sample size    I    and linear to the number of genes    G   .  We implemented the MMM method in the open-source R package MCube, which employs a carefully designed parallel computing framework to achieve high scalability and efficient memory control with user-configurable parameters. Notably, users can flexibly specify the number of worker processes along with the number of threads on Basic Linear Algebra Subprograms per worker. This hierarchical control empowers the maximal utilization of available computational resources. In addition, to address memory constraints inherent in large-scale kernel matrix operations, we handle matrices that are too large to load in memory through memory-mapping to binary files on disk ( 91 ). This approach enables the kernel matrices that exceed physical memory limits while maintaining efficient data access.  We also conducted a runtime benchmark on simulated datasets (  SI Appendix , Fig. S2 ) and recorded the computation time for all the real data analyses (  SI Appendix , Table S7 ). evidence_text:
Randomized Spatial PCA (RASP): A computationally efficient method for dimensionality reduction of high-resolution spatial transcriptomics data. Spatial transcriptomics (ST) provides critical insights into the spatial organization of gene expression, enabling researchers to unravel the intricate relationship between cellular environments and biological function. Identifying spatial domains within tissues is key to understanding tissue architecture and mechanisms underlying development and disease progression. Here, we present Randomized Spatial PCA (RASP), a novel spatially-aware dimensionality reduction method for ST data. RASP is designed to be orders-of-magnitude faster than existing techniques, scale to datasets with 100,000+ locations, support flexible integration of non-transcriptomic covariates, and reconstruct de-noised, spatially-smoothed gene expression values. RASP itself is not a clustering or domain detection method; cell types and spatial regions are obtained by clustering the RASP PCs, and the effective cluster resolution depends on the K-nearest-neighbor (kNN) graph and a smoothing parameter β. It employs a randomized two-stage PCA framework and configurable spatial smoothing. RASP was compared to BASS, GraphST, SEDR, SpatialPCA, STAGATE, and CellCharter using diverse ST datasets (10x Visium, Stereo-Seq, MERFISH, 10x Xenium) on human and mouse tissues. In these benchmarks, RASP delivers comparable or superior accuracy in tissue-domain detection while achieving substantial improvements in computational speed. Its efficiency not only reduces runtime and resource requirements but also makes it practical to explore a broad range of spatial-smoothing parameters in a high-throughput fashion. By enabling rapid re-analysis under different parameter settings, RASP empowers users to fine-tune the balance between resolution and noise suppression on large, high-resolution subcellular datasets-a critical capability when investigating complex tissue architecture. 1 Introduction  Spatial transcriptomics (ST) assays measure gene expression at thousands to hundreds-of-thousands of spatial locations within tissue sections, enabling researchers to characterize transcript abundance  in vivo  at resolutions ranging from small cell groups to subcellular structures [ 1 , 2 ]. ST technology facilitates exploration of gene expression’s spatial organization, providing insights into cellular microenvironments and functional states of individual cells [ 3 ]. Two primary types of ST technologies exist: high-throughput sequencing technologies (sST) and single-molecule fluorescent in situ hybridization or imaging-based technologies (iST) [ 4 , 5 ]. High-throughput sequencing technologies, such as 10x Genomics’ Visium, capture mRNA transcripts at multiple sites or spots on a slide. The original 10x Visium platform offers a resolution of 55 μm, with each spot containing between 1–10 cells [ 6 ]. Emerging technologies, such as Visium HD, promise higher resolution of 2 μm, approaching single-cell levels, while alternatives like Slide-seq V2 capture transcripts with a resolution of 10 μm, also nearing single-cell resolution [ 7 , 8 ]. iST technologies, which reach single cell resolution, include platforms such as MERFISH/MERSCOPE (Vizgen), seqFISH, osmFISH, COsMx, and 10x Xenium [ 9 – 13 ]. A notable advancement in recent years has been the introduction of iST platforms that support the use of formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue samples, allowing for use of archival tissue banks, increasing the use of iST technologies [ 9 , 12 , 13 ]. Additionally, probe sets used in iST technologies have expanded, with systems like 10x Xenium developing probe sets with up to 5,000 individual probes [ 14 ], helping to bridge the trade-off between sequencing depth and resolution, allowing for more comprehensive profiling of gene expression. As costs decrease and technology advances, ST studies will transition from analyzing small sample sizes to extensive investigations [ 2 , 13 , 15 – 19 ], generating multi-slide, multi-timepoint ST datasets covering millions of cells across tissues [ 2 ]. Larger datasets necessitate development of novel computational tools that leverage both spatial and transcriptomic data efficiently.  ST’s rich data allows tissue investigation at multiple biological scales, and computational approaches must adapt. On a cellular level, cell type assignment facilitates spatial distribution visualization, enhancing understanding of cellular structure and function in native tissue [ 20 , 21 ]. At larger scales, identifying tissue domains aids comprehension of complex tissue microenvironments. Identifying cell types and spatial domains is achievable with recently developed computational tools. Popular single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) tools like Seurat and Scanpy perform principal component analysis (PCA) and clustering on gene expression data while disregarding spatial context [ 22 , 23 ]. However, methods like BASS and FICT now incorporate spatial context [ 24 , 25 ]. For spatial domain detection, methodologies like BASS, SEDR, GraphST, STAGATE, SpatialPCA, CellCharter, Banksy, NichePCA, and MENDER exist [ 24 , 26 – 33 ], utilizing different model architectures to embed spatial and gene expression data into low-dimensionality spaces for clustering and domain identification. ST data is often sparse, so de-noising and imputing poorly captured transcripts is a desirable feature, and many spatially-aware methods provide this functionality [ 34 – 37 ].  Yet, current methods have notable limitations. While effective on a single ST slice with few profiled locations, they are computationally intensive, exhibiting slow processing locally, particularly on iST datasets exceeding 100,000 cells. With iST dataset generation increasing, efficient, spatially-aware dimensionality reduction algorithms are necessary. Many existing methods are also inflexible and complex, hindering customization of spatial smoothing or integration of non-transcriptomic covariates, such as those derived from hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) images or protein abundance metrics. Addressing these limitations is crucial for deriving meaningful insights from large-scale ST datasets.  In this paper, we present Randomized Spatial PCA (RASP), a computational method for fast, scalable, and interpretable spatially aware dimensionality reduction. RASP produces spatially smoothed principal components (PCs) and optionally reconstructs de-noised, spatially smoothed gene expression values. Importantly, RASP is not a clustering or domain detection method; cell types and spatial domains are obtained by clustering the RASP PCs with standard algorithms (e.g., Leiden/Louvain or Gaussian mixtures). The effective resolution of the resulting clusters depends on the K-nearest-neighbor (kNN) graph used to define spatial neighborhoods and a smoothing parameter  β  that controls the strength of spatial regularization. RASP employs randomized linear algebra for efficient dimensionality reduction of large datasets [ 38 ], supports integration of covariates such as cellular density or sequencing depth, and exposes the kNN and  β  parameters to enable user-controlled smoothing. Tested on six real-world and two simulated datasets (including 10x Genomics Visium/Visium HD and subcellular imaging technologies like Stereo-Seq, MERFISH, and 10x Xenium), pipelines that cluster RASP PCs exhibit tissue-domain concordance comparable to or better than existing methods, with competitive runtimes. This efficiency facilitates systematic exploration of kNN and  β  to balance resolution and noise suppression in large, high-resolution datasets.    2 Results   2.1 RASP overview  RASP performs dimensionality reduction and spatial smoothing on normalized ST data, with the option to incorporate location-specific covariates. The randomized two-stage PCA optimizes computational efficiency while enabling the flexible and efficient analysis of high-resolution ST datasets. RASP utilizes a sparse matrix representation of the data, conducts dimensionality reduction via randomized PCA, and generates spatially smoothed principal components (PCs) using an inverse distance matrix sparsified by a kNN threshold ( Fig 1A ). Covariates integration into the latent variables is achieved through an optional second-stage randomized PCA on a matrix with covariates appended to first-stage smoothed PCs. RASP’s spatially smoothed PCs are useful for a variety of downstream analyses, including clustering and reduced rank reconstruction of gene-level expression. See Algorithm 1 and  Sect 4.1  for details. Note that by default, RASP utilizes 20 PCs, although this is a user tunable parameter. See Figs O and P in  S1 Text  for effects of variable PC number on RASP performance.   10.1371/journal.pcbi.1013759.g001  Fig 1   RASP overview.   A: Method workflow.  RASP takes as input a cell-by-gene expression matrix and spatial coordinates from a ST experiment. The expression matrix is reduced via randomized PCA and spatially smoothed by a sparse inverse distance matrix. Non-transcriptomic covariates can be added at this stage (not pictured). The output of RASP can be used for downsteam analyses including cell type annotation and spatial domain identification.  B: Evaluation pipeline.  RASP was tested on four ST datasets each generated using a different platform (MERFISH, Stereo-seq, Xenium, Visium) and two synthetic datasets (100 replicates each). RASP results were compared against standard PCA and alternative spatially aware dimensionality reduction models (SEDR, GraphST, SpatialPCA, BASS, STAGATE, CellCharter, nichePCA, and MENDER) for both accuracy and speed.                        2.2 RASP evaluation on real data  We applied RASP to six publicly available ST datasets generated using diverse techniques and resolutions on human and mouse tissues including the mouse ovary (Vizgen MERFISH technology), mouse olfactory bulb (STOmics Stereo-seq technology), human breast cancer tumor (10x Genomics, Xenium technology), human DLPFC (10x Genomics, Visium technology), and Sagittal mouse brain (Allen institute, Vizgen MERFISH technology), and Coronal mouse brain (10x Genomics, Visium HD technology) ( Fig 1B ) and Table A in  S1 Text . For details regarding field standard preprocessing steps used, such as filtering criteria and normalization, please refer to  Sects 4.4.1 – 4.4.3 , and Sect 1.2 in  S1 Text , Sect 1.3 in  S1 Text .  We evaluated RASP by clustering locations using the spatially smoothed principal components (PCs) output by the algorithm. Cluster quality was assessed through spatial continuity and compactness via CHAOS score (Sect 1.3 in  S1 Text ), spatial autocorrelation by Moran’s I (Sect 1.4 in  S1 Text ) and the Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) (Sect 1.2 in  S1 Text ) compared to ground truth annotations. We conducted a sensitivity analysis on key RASP parameters, focusing on the kNN distance threshold for sparse matrix construction and the  β  power parameter (see  Fig 1A ). This analysis used a one-parameter-at-a-time strategy to investigate parameter impacts on clustering outcomes.  Performance comparisons were made between RASP and eight other spatially informed dimensionality reduction or spatial domain detection methods: GraphST [ 26 ], SEDR [ 27 ], SpatialPCA [ 28 ], STAGATE [ 29 ], CellCharter [ 30 ], nichePCA [ 32 ],MENDER [ 33 ], and the joint cell type and domain inference tool BASS [ 24 ], as well as performing randomized PCA. For each comparison, we adhered to the respective GitHub tutorials to run these methods for spatial domain and cell type detection. Clustering of latent dimensions found by each method was performed using Mclust [ 39 ], Louvain [ 40 ], Leiden [ 41 ], and Walktrap [ 42 ] algorithms, where appropriate. For datasets with ground truth annotations, the ARI values presented are based on a default range of parameters outlined in  Table 1 .   10.1371/journal.pcbi.1013759.t001  Table 1   RASP parameter suggestions.                                  Technology  Label type  
 
                      β    
 
  kNN Default  kNN Range  Moran’s I Value  CHAOS Value      Visium  small/cell type  2  1  1-2  smaller is better  larger is better    Visium  spatial domain  0  5  3-10  larger is better  smaller is better    All others  small/cell type  2  10  2-20  smaller is better  larger is better    All others  spatial domain  0  50  30-100  larger is better  smaller is better         2.3 RASP identifies biologically relevant structures in heterogeneous mouse ovary MERFISH data  We selected this MERFISH dataset for its complex tissue structure and cellular resolution, featuring spatially distinct and heterogeneous cell types. Obtained from Huang et al., [ 15 ] it includes ground truth cell type annotations based on histology and differential gene expression ( Fig 2A ). Annotations include luteal, luteinizing mural, granulosa, theca, and stromal cells, with clear spatial organization, alongside endothelial, epithelial, and immune cells, which are heterogeneously distributed across the tissue [ 43 ], making automatic annotation challenging.   10.1371/journal.pcbi.1013759.g002  Fig 2   Overview of mouse ovary analysis using Vizgen MERFISH data.   A: Cell Type Annotation Comparison  — Ground truth cell type labels (top left) alongside predictions from RASP, normal PCA, and other methods, illustrating accuracy of spatial mapping.  B: Runtime Performance  — Comparison of computational runtime across methods, highlighting RASP efficiency relative to standard randomized PCA.  C: Clustering Accuracy (ARI) Across Methods  — Adjusted Rand Index values for all methods, color-coded by clustering algorithm; shows full ARI range at default RASP parameters (kNN = 2–20,                        β  =  2     ) and the default parameter single-point performance (kNN=10,                        β  =  2     ). RASP-moran and RASP-CHAOS indicate ARI when using label-agnostic metrics for parameter selection.  D: Effect of  β  on ARI  — ARI values for RASP with inverse distance weighting raised to                        β  =  0      (left), 0.5 (middle), and 2 (right), plotted against kNN values; colors indicate clustering algorithm used.  E: Spatial Expression Patterns of Lhcgr  — Normalized expression (left), reduced rank reconstructed expression (center), and spatially smoothed reconstructed expression (right). White lines depict luteinizing mural and luteal cell compartment boundaries.                      For this dataset, RASP was set at the recommended ‘cell type’ default parameters ( Table 1 ), using Louvain clustering achieved the highest ARI of 0.69, followed by standard randomized PCA (ARI of 0.58) and SEDR (ARI of 0.57) (see  Fig 2A  and  2C ). We employed three different clustering methods to assign labels to cells in the dataset. The Louvain and Leiden algorithms performed best, while Mclust the worst. Note that BASS utilizes Mclust as a prior in its Bayesian framework but does not support a user-defined clustering algorithm.  Relative runtimes show RASP is comparable to nichePCA and one to three orders-of-magnitude faster than other methods on this dataset ( Fig 2B ). RASP performance diminishes with increasing kNN thresholds for PC smoothing; highlighted by decreasing ARI scores at larger kNN values ( Fig 2D ). This result is not unexpected for cell type annotation, but it is noteworthy that the local smoothing employed by RASP in this task, essentially borrowing information from just a few neighboring cells, has a marked increase in performance over normal randomized PCA for clustering-based label assignment.  Using RASP-based reduced rank reconstruction of gene expression profiles, we analyzed the spatial signature of the luteinizing hormone/chorionic gonadotropin receptor (Lhcgr) given its essential role in ovarian biology and ovulation ( Fig 2E ). Lhcgr is a G-protein coupled receptor that binds luteinizing hormone and chorionic gonadotropins, is expressed on luteal and luteinizing mural cells [ 44 – 46 ]. Originally, gene expression showed minimal spatial patterning ( Fig 2E , left plot). After PCA-based reduced rank and RASP-based reconstruction, expression is localized to luteinizing mural and luteal cells ( Fig 2E , middle and right plots). When comparing normalized expression log-2 fold-change (log2FC) between luteinizing mural and luteal cells vs all other cells, both PCA-based and RASP-based reduced rank reconstruction results in higher mean expression (localization) to these regions. For this example, we utilized the distances to only 2 kNN, which results in very similar log2FC values between normal PCA-based and RASP-based reconstruction.    2.4 Incorporation of covariates improves RASP annotation of mouse ovary ST data  A key feature of RASP is support for integrating non-transcriptomic covariates into reduced dimensions. The two-stage PCA approach used by RASP offers benefits relative to a single PCA on merged transcriptomic and non-transcriptomic variables: It prevents covariate signal dilution within the high-dimensional transcriptomic data, and decouples spatial smoothing of expression data from covariates. To RASP covariate integration, we explored three covariates on analysis of the mouse ovary data: local cell density, transcriptomic library size, and cell volume. For each covariate, we performed parameter sweeps over the kNN parameter using three RASP configurations: 1) standard RASP without covariates (i.e., the configuration used to generate the results in  Fig 2 ) 2), single-stage RASP executed on the merge of ST expression data and covariates, and 3) two-stage RASP with the covariates added to the output from the first stage (see  Fig 3A  (bottom)). For these experiments, the  β  parameter was held constant at                 β  =  2     ,the optimal value for this dataset (see  Figs 2D , and A in  S1 Text ). For local density, spatial smoothing was applied to the PC matrix, but not the local density variable, as local density inherently contains spatial information. Library size and cell volume covariates where smoothed by the same inverse distance weighting as the PCs matrix, prior the second round of dimensionality reduction. In addition, we explored the addition of local cell density for the breast cancer dataset, using the same experimental design described above, with the RASP kNN held constant at 60. The results were less striking than the ovary dataset, with both the one and two stage versions improving the clustering across some of the density values for Louvain clustering, and generally reducing performance when using Leiden or mclust algorithms (Fig F in  S1 Text ).   10.1371/journal.pcbi.1013759.g003  Fig 3   Covariate analysis (Mouse ovary dataset) for the cell type clustering case.   A: RASP Model Architectures  — Illustrations of the baseline RASP model without covariates (top left), the single-stage RASP model incorporating one covariate (top right), and the two-stage RASP model architecture that integrates an additional covariate for improved clustering.  B: Clustering Performance with Covariate Adjustment  — Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) comparisons across models without covariates, single-stage RASP, and two-stage RASP, plotted against varying kNN values. Columns represent different clustering algorithms, while rows show the impact of different covariates: local cell density (top), cell library size (middle), and cell volume (bottom).                      All three covariates impacted ARI values, with clustering algorithm determining the magnitude and direction of the ARI change. The ARI using Mclust improved regardless of covariate added, with single-stage RASP equal to or outperforming the two-stage version in most cases (see  Fig 3B  (left column)). Mclust ARI without covariates was 0.39 improving to 0.45 and 0.5 with covariates. ARI with Leiden was highest for two-stage RASP in most cases, while the single-stage version was covariate dependent, with the addition of local density decreasing ARI values, and addition of library size and cell volume having mixed effects (see  Fig 3B  (right column)). Notably, Leiden clustering with cell volume achieved a top ARI of 0.71, the highest attained for this dataset. Louvain- classification was hindered by local density additions, with ARI values dropping across kNN values, while affects of library size and cell volume fluctuated with kNN value. Overall, these results indicate that integration of covariates can enhance RASP performance, but covariate selection, clustering algorithm, spatial smoothing require careful consideration. Importantly, two-stage RASP avoids covariate dilution offering more control over spatial smoothing.    2.5 RASP enables the investigation of tissue domains at multiple scales  To investigate RASP’s capacity to resolve tissue structure across a hierarchy of physical scales, we applied it to the Allen Institute’s adult mouse brain atlas [ 47 ],[ 1 ]. This dataset provides comprehensive cell type annotations as well as multiple hierarchical levels of cortical and subcortical brain region annotations, allowing for the assessment of both fine-grained (cell type) and coarse-grained (anatomical region) clustering performance. We compared RASP followed by clustering to the ground truth cell type labels and broad brain region divisions ( Fig 4A  and  4D ). For the cell typing task, RASP achieved an ARI of 0.77, substantially surpassing other state-of-the-art clustering methods, using cell type parameter defaults (                β  =  2.0     , kNN=2–20;  Fig 4B , and  4C ). For the higher-level regional annotation task, RASP achieved an ARI of 0.55, outperforming other approaches with parameters designed for large-scale spatial domains (                β  =  0     , kNN=50–100;  Fig 4E  and  4H ). See  Sect 2.11  for further discussion on RASP’s multi-scale ability.   10.1371/journal.pcbi.1013759.g004  Fig 4   RASP performance across different scales in the Mouse Sagittal brain dataset. B: Cell Type Clustering Accuracy  — Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) values for all methods based on cell type annotations in  A , with colors indicating clustering algorithms. Displays full ARI range at default RASP parameters (kNN = 2–20,                        β  =  2     ) and single-point ARI for default parameters (kNN = 10,                        β  =  2     ). RASP-moran and RASP-CHAOS indicate ARI when using label-agnostic metrics for parameter selection. RASP-moran and RASP-CHAOS indicate ARI when using label-agnostic metrics for parameter selection.
